Primary Reason for Home Health / Medical Necessity
The patient is considered homebound because she is an 80-year-old female with severe functional and medical impairments that prevent safe, independent leaving of the home without
assistance. Objective findings from the record document morbid obesity (BMI 52.4), bilateral primary osteoarthritis of the knees with marked functional decline, bilateral lower-extremity
lymphedema/venous stasis with dependent edema, painful bilateral foot calluses, chronic hypoxic respiratory failure/COPD (on supplemental oxygen as needed), and chronic kidney disease
stage 3. Functionally she uses a walker and reports she is only able to ambulate a few steps, is unable to stand for more than a few seconds, becomes short of breath and experiences
exertional lower-extremity pain with ambulation, and requires a power chair for intra-home mobility (current power chair is too large for safe use at home and she is in the process of obtaining
an appropriately sized chair). She is able to transfer but has very limited ability to ambulate and is at high fall risk due to extreme obesity, knee osteoarthritis, lymphedema, neuropathic pain, and
deconditioning. The patient lives alone with no live-in caregiver (friend provides near-daily assistance), which increases safety risk if she were to leave home and require unassisted ambulation
or recovery from exertion. Leaving the home requires a considerable and taxing effort because even minimal ambulation provokes dyspnea and lower-extremity pain, she can only take a few
steps and cannot sustain standing long enough to perform transfers or traverse typical community distances, and her current mobility equipment is inadequate for safe community ambulation.
Exiting the home would therefore require either multiple persons and/or specialized equipment (appropriately sized power chair and transport) and poses significant fall and respiratory risk.
Allowable, limited absences are restricted to medically necessary appointments or brief tolerated activities with appropriate transport and assistance; prolonged or frequent community outings
would exceed her functional tolerance and increase risk of decompensation. Given these documented impairments (morbid obesity, bilateral knee osteoarthritis, bilateral lower-extremity
lymphedema with dependent edema, chronic hypoxic respiratory failure/COPD, CKD stage 3, painful foot calluses, inability to ambulate more than a few steps, requirement for power mobility,
and lack of a live-in caregiver), skilled home health services are medically necessary to provide skilled nursing for medication adjustment and renal monitoring after diuretic change, supervised
management of oxygen therapy and respiratory symptoms, and skilled PT/OT for strengthening, gait/transfer training, power-chair assessment and training, and fall-risk mitigation, as well as
skilled wound/podiatry coordination for foot calluses-services required to restore or maintain function and to safely manage her chronic medical conditions at home.

GG0100. Prior Functioning: Everyday Activities: Indicate the patient's usual ability with everyday activities prior to the current illness, exacerbation, or injury.
3. Independent - Patient completed the activities by him/herself, with or without an assistive device, with no assistance from a helper

1. Dependent - A helper completed the activities for the patient.
A. Self Care: Code the patient's need for assistance with bathing, dressing, using the toilet, or
eating prior to the current illness, exacerbation, or injur
B. Indoor Mobility (Ambulation): Code the patient's need for assistance with walking from room
to room (with or without a device such as cane, crutch or walker) prior to the current illness,
C. Stairs: Code the patient's need for assistance with internal or external stairs (with or without a
device such as cane, crutch, or walker) prior to the current illness, exacerbation or injury.
D. Functional Cognition: Code the patient's need for assistance with planning regular tasks, such

Indicate devices and aids used by the patient prior to the current illness, exacerbation, or injury (Check all that apply)
Code the patient's usual performance at SOC/ROC for each activity using the 6-point scale. If activity was not attempted at SOC/ROC, code the reason.
Safety and Quality of Performance - If helper assistance is required because patient's performance is unsafe or of poor quality, score according to amount of assistance provided.
Activities may be completed with or without assistive devices.
06. Independent - Patient completes the activity by him/herself with no assistance from a helper.
05. Setup or clean-up assistance - Helper sets up or cleans up; patient completes activity. Helper assists only prior to or following the activity.
04. Supervision or touching assistance - Helper provides verbal cues and/or touching/steadying and/or contact guard assistance as patient completes activity. Assistance may be provided
03. Partial/moderate assistance - Helper does LESS THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts, holds or supports trunk or limbs, but provides less than half the effort.
02. Substantial/maximal assistance - Helper does MORE THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts or holds trunk or limbs and provides more than half the effort.
01. Dependent - Helper does ALL of the effort. Patient does none of the effort to complete the activity. Or, the assistance of 2 or more helpers is required for the patient to complete the activity.
If activity was not attempted, code reason:
09. Not applicable - Not attempted and the patient did not perform this activity prior to the current illness, exacerbation or injury.
10. Not attempted due to environmental limitations (e.g., lack of equipment, weather constraints)
88. Not attempted due to medical conditions or safety concerns

Patient currently relies on a motorized wheelchair for ambulation and transfer and requires assistance/supervision in ADL's/IADL's. A cane and walker do not meet her needs as it does not provide
enough support, and the patient is unable to ambulate more than a few steps to transfer, making her a fall risk. She can stand from a seated position without assistance and get on her current power
chair. She gets short of breath with ambulation and can only ambulate a few steps due to exertion as well as lower extremity pain.
M1306. Does this patient have at least one Unhealed Pressure Ulcer/Injury at Stage 2 or Higher or designated as Unstageable?
(Excludes Stage 1 pressure injuries and all healed pressure ulcers/injuries)

M1311. Current Number of Unhealed Pressure Ulcers/Injuries at Each Stage
A1. Stage 2: Partial thickness loss of dermis presenting as a shallow open ulcer with a red or pink wound bed, without slough. May also present as an intact
or open/ruptured blister. Number of Stage 2 pressure ulcers
B1. Stage 3: Full thickness tissue loss. Subcutaneous fat may be visible but bone, tendon, or muscle is not exposed. Slough may be present but does not
obscure the depth of tissue loss. May include undermining and tunneling. Number of Stage 3 pressure ulcers
C1. Stage 4: Full thickness tissue loss with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle. Slough or eschar may be present on some parts of the wound bed. Often
includes undermining and tunneling. Number of Stage 4 pressure ulcers
D1. Unstageable: Non-removable dressing/device: Known but not stageable due to removable dressing/device Number of unstageable pressure
E1. Unstageable: Slough and/or eschar: Known but not stageable due to coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar Number of unstageable
pressure ulcers due to coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar
F1. Unstageable: Deep tissue injury Number of unstageable pressure injuries presenting as deep tissue injury

(M1740) Cognitive, behavioral, and psychiatric symptoms that are demonstrated at least once a week (Reported or Observed): (Mark all that apply.
1 Memory deficit: failure to recognize familiar persons/places, inability to recall events of past 24 hours, significant memory loss so that supervision is required
2- Impaired decision-making: failure to perform usual ADLs or IADLs, inability to appropriately stop activities, jeopardizes safety through actions
3 - Verbal disruption: yelling, threatening, excessive profanity, sexual references, etc.
4 - Physical aggression: aggressive or combative to self and others (for example, hits self, throws objects, punches, dangerous maneuvers with wheelchair or other objects)
5 - Disruptive, infantile, or socially inappropriate behavior (excludes verbal actions)
7- None of the above behaviors demonstrated
(M1745) Frequency of Disruptive Behavior Symptoms (Reported or Observed):
Current ability to tend safely to personal hygiene needs (specifically: washing face and hands, hair care, shaving or make up, teeth or denture care, or fingernail care)
0 - Able to groom self unaided, with or without the use of assistive devices or adapted methods.
1 - Grooming utensils must be placed within reach before able to complete grooming activities.
2- Someone must assist the patient to groom self.
3 - Patient depends entirely upon someone else for grooming needs
Current Ability to Dress Upper Body safely (with or without dressing aids) including undergarments, pullovers, front-opening shirts and blouses, managing zippers, buttons, and snaps:
0 Able to get clothes out of closets and drawers, put them on and remove them from the upper body without assistance.
1 - Able to dress upper body without assistance if clothing is laid out or handed to the patient.
2 - Someone must help the patient put on upper body clothing.
3 - Patient depends entirely upon another person to dress the upper body.
Current Ability to Dress Lower Body safely (with or without dressing aids) including undergarments, slacks, socks or nylons, shoes:
0 - Able to obtain, put on, and remove clothing and shoes without assistance.
1 - Able to dress lower body without assistance if clothing and shoes are laid out or handed to the patient.
2 - Someone must help the patient put on undergarments, slacks, socks or nylons, and shoes.
3 - Patient depends entirely upon another person to dress lower body.
Current ability to wash entire body safely. Excludes grooming (washing face, washing hands, and shampooing hair).
0 - Able to bathe self in shower or tub independently, including getting in and out of tub/shower.
1- With the use of devices, is able to bathe self in shower or tub independently, including getting in and out of the tub/shower.
2 - Able to bathe in shower or tub with the intermittent assistance of another person: (a) for intermittent supervision or encouragement or reminders, OR (b) to get in and out of
the shower or tub, OR (c) for washing difficult to reach areas.
3 - Able to participate in bathing self in shower or tub, but requires presence of another person throughout the bath for assistance or supervision.
4 - Unable to use the shower or tub, but able to bathe self independently with or without the use of devices at the sink, in chair, or on commode.
5 - Unable to use the shower or tub, but able to participate in bathing self in bed, at the sink, in bedside chair, or on commode, with the assistance or supervision of another
6 - Unable to participate effectively in bathing and is bathed totally by another person.
Current ability to get to and from the toilet or bedside commode safely and transfer on and off toilet/commode
0 - Able to get to and from the toilet and transfer independently with or without a device

0 - Able to manage toileting hygiene and clothing management without assistance.
1- Able to manage toileting hygiene and clothing management without assistance if supplies/implements are laid out for the patient.

3 - Patient depends entirely upon another person to maintain toileting hygiene
Current ability to move safely from bed to chair, or ability to turn and position self in bed if patient is bedfast
1- Able to transfer with minimal human assistance or with use of an assistive device.

3 - Unable to transfer self and is unable to bear weight or pivot when transferred by another person

Current ability to walk safely, once in a standing position, or use a wheelchair, once in a seated position, on a variety of surfaces.
0 Able to independently walk on even and uneven surfaces and negotiate stairs with or without railings (specifically: needs no human assistance or assistive device).

3 - Able to walk only with the supervision or assistance of another person at all times.

6 - Bedfast, unable to ambulate or be up in a chair.
Code the patient's usual performance at SOC/ROC for each activity using the 6-point scale. If activity was not attempted at SOC/ROC, code the reason.
Safety and Quality of Performance If helper assistance is required because patient's performance is unsafe or of poor quality, score according to amount of assistance provided.
Activities may be completed with or without assistive devices.
06. Independent Patient completes the activity by him/herself with no assistance from a helper.
05. Setup or clean-up assistance - Helper sets up or cleans up; patient completes activity. Helper assists only prior to or following the activity.
04. Supervision or touching assistance - Helper provides verbal cues and/or touching/steadying and/or contact guard assistance as patient
provided throughout the activity or intermittently.
03. Partial/moderate assistance - Helper does LESS THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts, holds or supports trunk or limbs, but provides less than half the effort.
02. Substantial/maximal assistance - Helper does MORE THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts or holds trunk or limbs and provides more than half the effort.
01. Dependent - Helper does ALL of the effort. Patient does none of the effort to complete the activity. Or, the assistance of 2 or more helpers is required for the patient to complete the activity.
If activity was not attempted, code reason:
09. Not applicable - Not attempted and the patient did not perform this activity prior to the current illness, exacerbation or injury.
10. Not attempted due to environmental limitations (e.g., lack of equipment, weather constraints)
88. Not attempted due to medical conditions or safety concerns
A. Eating: The ability to use suitable utensils to bring food and/or liquid to the mouth and swallow food and/or liquid once the meal is placed before the patient.
B. Oral Hygiene: The ability to use suitable items to clean teeth. Dentures (if applicable): The ability to insert and remove dentures into and from mouth, and manage denture soaking and rinsing with
C. Toileting Hygiene: The ability to maintain perineal hygiene, adjust clothes before and after voiding or having a bowel movement. If managing an ostomy, include wiping the opening but not
E. Shower/bathe self: The ability to bathe self, including washing, rinsing, and drying self (excludes washing of back and hair). Does not include transferring in/out of tub/shower.
F. Upper body dressing: The ability to dress and undress above the waist; including fasteners, if applicable.
G. Lower body dressing: The ability to dress and undress below the waist, including fasteners; does not include footwear.
H. Putting on/taking off footwear: The ability to put on and take off socks and shoes or other footwear that is appropriate for safe mobility; including fasteners, if applicable.

List each diagnosis for which the patient is receiving home care in Column 1, and enter its ICD-10-CM code at the level of highest specificity in Column 2 (diagnosis codes only - no surgical or
procedure codes allowed). Diagnoses are listed in the order that best reflects the seriousness of each condition and supports the disciplines and services provided. Rate the degree of symptom
control for each condition in Column 2. ICD-10-CM sequencing requirements must be followed if multiple coding is indicated for any diagnoses.
Code each row according to the following directions for each column:
Column 1: Enter the description of the diagnosis. Sequencing of diagnoses should reflect the seriousness of each condition and support the disciplines and services provided. Column 2: Enter
the ICD-10-CM code for the condition described in Column 1 - no surgical or procedure codes allowed. Codes must be entered at the level of highest specificity and ICD-10-CM coding rules and
sequencing requirements must be followed. Note that external cause codes (ICD-10-CM codes beginning with V, W, X, or Y) may not be reported in M1021 (Primary Diagnosis) but may be
reported in M1023 (Secondary Diagnoses). Also note that when a Z-code is reported in
Column 2 the code for the underlying condition can often be entered in Column 2, as long as it is an active on-going condition impacting home health care. Rate the degree of symptom control
for the condition listed in Column 1. Do not assign a symptom control rating if the diagnosis code is a V, W, X, Y or Z-code. Choose one value that represents the degree of symptom control
appropriate for each diagnosis using the following scale: 0 Asymptomatic, no treatment needed at this time 1 Symptoms well controlled with current therapy 2- Symptoms controlled with
difficulty, affecting daily functioning; patient needs ongoing monitoring 3- Symptoms poorly controlled; patient needs frequent adjustment in treatment and dose monitoring 4 - Symptoms poorly
controlled; history of re-hospitalizations Note that the rating for symptom control in Column 2 should not be used to determine the sequencing of the diagnoses listed in Column 1. These are
separate items and sequencing may not coincide.

The patient is considered homebound because she is an 80-year-old female with severe functional and medical impairments that prevent safe, independent leaving of the home without
assistance. Objective findings from the record document morbid obesity (BMI 52.4), bilateral primary osteoarthritis of the knees with marked functional decline, bilateral lower-extremity
lymphedema/venous stasis with dependent edema, painful bilateral foot calluses, chronic hypoxic respiratory failure/COPD (on supplemental oxygen as needed), and chronic kidney disease
stage 3. Functionally she uses a walker and reports she is only able to ambulate a few steps, is unable to stand for more than a few seconds, becomes short of breath and experiences
exertional lower-extremity pain with ambulation, and requires a power chair for intra-home mobility (current power chair is too large for safe use at home and she is in the process of obtaining
an appropriately sized chair). She is able to transfer but has very limited ability to ambulate and is at high fall risk due to extreme obesity, knee osteoarthritis, lymphedema, neuropathic pain,
and deconditioning. The patient lives alone with no live-in caregiver (friend provides near-daily assistance), which increases safety risk if she were to leave home and require unassisted
ambulation or recovery from exertion. Leaving the home requires a considerable and taxing effort because even minimal ambulation provokes dyspnea and lower-extremity pain, she can only
take a few steps and cannot sustain standing long enough to perform transfers or traverse typical community distances, and her current mobility equipment is inadequate for safe community
ambulation. Exiting the home would therefore require either multiple persons and/or specialized equipment (appropriately sized power chair and transport) and poses significant fall and
respiratory risk. Allowable, limited absences are restricted to medically necessary appointments or brief tolerated activities with appropriate transport and assistance; prolonged or frequent
community outings would exceed her functional tolerance and increase risk of decompensation. Given these documented impairments (morbid obesity, bilateral knee osteoarthritis, bilateral
lower-extremity lymphedema with dependent edema, chronic hypoxic respiratory failure/COPD, CKD stage 3, painful foot calluses, inability to ambulate more than a few steps, requirement for
power mobility, and lack of a live-in caregiver), skilled home health services are medically necessary to provide skilled nursing for medication adjustment and renal monitoring after diuretic
change, supervised management of oxygen therapy and respiratory symptoms, and skilled PT/OT for strengthening, gait/transfer training, power-chair assessment and training, and fall-risk
mitigation, as well as skilled wound/podiatry coordination for foot calluses-services required to restore or maintain function and to safely manage her chronic medical conditions at home.
HH SN frequency: 2 times a week for 1 week, 1 time a week for 3 weeks for skilled observation and assessment, medication reconciliation, pain assessment, wound monitoring and
management, infection control measures, diet, home safety and disease process education.
HH PT frequency: For evaluation and treatment for mobility, gait, transfer, establish therapeutic exercises, strengthening, home safety and fall prevention techniques training.
Oxygen Therapy Order: Continuous Oxygen at 3LPM via nasal cannula for shortness of breath.
-Patient will demonstrate safe use of walker and power chair with fewer verbal cues, showing improved transfer ability and reduced fall risk within 4 weeks.
-Patient will maintain oxygen saturation ≥ 92% at rest and ≥ 90% with activity, as evidenced by skilled nursing monitoring and documentation within 4 weeks.
-Patient will verbalize understanding of diuretic regimen and renal monitoring needs, with adherence to fluid restriction and recognition of signs of imbalance within 4 weeks.
-Patient will report improved pain control in knees and feet, with pain reduced by at least 2 points on the pain scale during mobility or ADLs within 4 weeks.
-Patient will remain free from falls or falls-related injuries in the home, demonstrating increased safety awareness and consistent use of assistive devices within 4 weeks.
Elizabeth Root is an 80-year-old female patient, referred for home health care on March 26, 2026. The attending physician overseeing her care is Dr. Stacy Leifried. The assessment for home
health services was completed by Emiliya Berberova, RN, on March 30, 2026. It is noteworthy that Elizabeth was not discharged from any inpatient facility within the past 14 days, indicating a
direct referral for home health services without prior hospitalization.
Elizabeth is classified as homebound due to multiple factors. Her condition prevents her from leaving the home without significant effort. She experiences difficulty standing or walking for
extended periods and requires human assistance due to an unsteady gait and poor balance. Additionally, she finds leaving the home exhausting, often necessitating extended rest periods to
The primary reason for Elizabeth's need for home health services is her diagnosis of bilateral primary osteoarthritis of the knees (diagnostic code M17.0), which significantly impacts her mobility
and overall functional ability. In addition to this primary diagnosis, she has comorbidities that include lymphedema, chronic kidney disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), and
chronic respiratory failure with hypoxia. These conditions further complicate her health status and increase her need for skilled services.
A comprehensive head-to-toe assessment reveals several key findings. Cardiopulmonary evaluation indicates a heart rate of 64 beats per minute, a blood pressure of 149/78 mmHg, and a
respiratory rate of 16 breaths per minute, which suggests moderate cardiovascular and respiratory function but with some concerns due to her COPD and chronic respiratory issues.
Gastrointestinal assessment is unremarkable, but Elizabeth has reported occasional bowel incontinence. Integumentary assessment shows no acute skin issues, and safety assessments
indicate a need for assistance with activities of daily living due to her mobility limitations.
Psychosocially, Elizabeth lives alone and demonstrates no signs of depression or anxiety, as evidenced by her PHQ-2 score of 0. She appears alert and oriented, with adequate cognitive
function, and is able to engage in social interactions without notable difficulties. Her living arrangements are suitable, although her independence is limited by her physical capabilities.
Elizabeth's vital signs are stable, with a temperature of 96.9°F, which is within normal limits. Her cognitive function has been assessed through the Brief Interview for Mental Status (BIMS),
scoring 15, indicating no acute changes in her mental status. She remains oriented to person, place, time, and situation, with coherent thought processes and appropriate behavior during the
In terms of activities of daily living (ADLs), Elizabeth requires significant assistance. She can dress her upper body independently if clothing is prepared for her but needs help with lower body
dressing and transfers. She is chair-bound and cannot ambulate independently, although she can wheel herself with the use of a wheelchair. Her functional ability is classified as independent in
self-care but needing assistance for mobility and ADLs.
Elizabeth's mobility is severely limited, with reports of dyspnea upon moderate exertion, such as during dressing or transferring. She utilizes a walker for support and requires assistance with
transfers, indicating a need for skilled nursing interventions to help manage her mobility issues safely.
Socially, Elizabeth has reported limited access to transportation, which affects her ability to attend medical appointments and obtain necessary medications. This lack of support necessitates
regular home health visits to ensure her medical needs are met.
A thorough review of Elizabeth's medications reveals that she is currently taking five or more medications daily, including those for hypertension, diabetes, and her chronic conditions. Attention
needs to be given to potential drug interactions and adherence to her medication regimen due to her reported history of difficulty complying with medical instructions.
Elizabeth's primary caregiver is not formally identified, but she does have informal support from neighbors and friends who help as needed. However, these supports may require training and

In conclusion, Elizabeth Root's medical necessity for skilled home health services is clear, given her primary diagnosis of bilateral osteoarthritis and multiple comorbidities that affect her mobility
and overall health. The need for skilled nursing visits is critical to manage her medications, assist with ADLs, and monitor her chronic conditions. A visit frequency of two to three times per week
is recommended to ensure comprehensive management of her care and to address her evolving health needs.

Explained Home Health service to patient/family/caregiver(s). Explained consent to care, release of liability, and patient rights & responsibilities. Consent to care, a liability release, and consent to
photograph were signed by the patient/responsible party. A comprehensive assessment on all body systems with OASIS elements evaluation was initiated. Reconciled facility discharges medications
against routine home prescriptions without issue. Discussed advance directives, privacy policy, COVID-19 prevention, 911 protocols, basic home safety, and fall prevention principles with

patient/family/caregiver(s). Discussed plan of care draft with patient and explained what to expect in the next visits and the remainder of the care episode. Also explained that the initial discussion on
the management of other disease processes will be done on the next visit.

Assessed patient's musculoskeletal status due to Osteoarthritis. Instructed patient for signs and symptoms such joint stiffness, swelling, pain which worsens with activities and is not relieved with rest,
progressive loss in the range of motion, crepitation, increasing weakness, fatigue, and increasing morning stiffness.
Instructed patient/caregiver on measures to alleviate edema such as elevating BLEs above the level of the heart while lying down or on a stool/chair while sitting.
Instructed patient to adhere and take pain medication before pain becomes severe to achieve pain control. Provided teachings on nonpharmacologic pain relief measures such as relaxation
techniques and positioning. Instructed patient/PCG to notify SN/MD if patient is experiencing pain level greater than 7/10, unable to tolerate pain, and if pain is affecting ability to perform normal
Assessed pt's safety in environment, mobility and functional limitations to reduce falls or accidents every visit. Assessed the patient's ability to perform ADLs effectively and safely every visit.
Instructed Patient/Caregiver on the importance of assessing conditions/risks that may contribute to occurrence of falls such as fatigue/ muscle weakness. Encouraged to perform exercises if tolerated
for strengthening and maintenance of posture and flexibility. Established HEP and educate Patient/Caregiver on passive/active assistive ROM exercises and encouraged to perform exercises to

Reinforced patient/caregiver on fall prevention techniques including use of proper assistive device, locking wheelchair before transfers, and placing patient's items within arm's reach. Stress to patient

to avoid attempting tasks that are too difficult for present level of functioning and ask for help when needed. Instructed the patient/caregiver to contact agency to report any fall with or without minor
injury and to call 911 for fall resulting in serious injury or causing severe pain or immobility. Instructed on safety measures to prevent falls: remove clutter from walkways. Secure loose rugs with
double-faced tape, tacks or a slip-resistant backing or remove loose rugs from home. Immediately clean spilled liquids, grease or food. Use nonslip mats inside bathtub or shower. Light up living
space. Keep home brightly lit to avoid tripping on objects that are hard to see. Place night lights in the bedroom, bathroom and hallways. Place a lamp within reach of the bed for middle-of-the-night
needs. Store flashlights in easy-to-find places in case of power outages. Use assistive devices such as walker, grab bars, commode. Wear properly fitting, sturdy shoes with nonskid soles.
Medication reconciliation was done during the visit. Patient/caregiver verbalized understanding in all teachings during the visit.
Patient verbalized 60% of understanding on the disease process taught during this visit. Patient unable to teach back complete understanding on fall precautions and hypertension urgency
symptoms. Patient also redemonstrated 60% of home safety and fall prevention measures using assistive device wheelchair as patient still demonstrates weakness, poor endurance and ambulation
difficulty. Patient and caregiver require further teaching.

Patient is an 80-year-old homebound female admitted to Avery Home Health Care. Patient has a known medical history of CKD stage 3, Hypertension, COPD, Chronic respiratory failure with
hypoxia, Atrial fibrillation, Hyperparathyroidism, Osteoarthritis of both knees, Chronic back pain, Lymphedema of both lower extremities, Morbid obesity, Left shoulder dislocation, Sleep apnea. The
patient lives alone with occasional help from her friend for assistance at home and during MD appointments. Patient complains of bilateral knee pain, interfering with functional status and impacting

activities of daily living. Patient has an unsteady gait, poor balance, and generalized weakness, placing her at risk for falls and injury. Patient is alert, oriented, able to make needs known, able to
follow instructions, cooperative, and verbally responsive. Patient has shortness of breath with moderate exertion. Skin is intact; no breakdown or impairment observed. Patient with severe
lymphedema on both lower extremities; no open areas were noted. Podiatry referral was made. Patient is homebound due to current health condition, unsafe to leave home, requires assistance to
complete ADLs/IADLs safely, and has a medical necessity to be under home health care to prevent potential complications of medical conditions. Patient currently relies on a motorized wheelchair for
ambulation and transfer and requires assistance/supervision in ADL's/IADL's. A cane and walker do not meet her needs as it does not provide enough support, and the patient is unable to ambulate
more than a few steps to transfer, making her a fall risk. She can stand from a seated position without assistance and get on her current power chair. She gets short of breath with ambulation and can
only ambulate a few steps due to exertion as well as lower extremity pain. The patient exhibits functional deficits as evidenced by generalized weakness, low endurance, low activity tolerance, poor
balance, and an unsteady gait, and requires assistive devices for safe mobility. Patient demonstrates a knowledge deficit related to disease processes and management, as well as medication
management and compliance. Patient will benefit from home health care services for observation and assessment of the patient's response to treatment, medication reconciliation, pain assessment,
and training and education on infection control measures, diet, fall and safety precautions, disease process, medications, and changes in clinical signs and symptoms. Skilled Nursing visits for
assessment and monitoring of vital signs, medication and diet compliance, pain status, and other symptoms related to patients' diagnosis.
Physical Therapy for evaluation and assessment of rehab needs and to establish a treatment plan and implement a home exercise program.
Discussed consent for services, plan of care, patient's rights/responsibilities, HIPAA policies, and Home Health agency numbers were provided. Patient verbalized understanding, and consent was
signed. Physical assessment, history taking, and vital signs were monitored. All home medications were reviewed and discussed, including indication, dose, frequency, schedule, possible side effects,
and precautions. SN educated the patient on safety with ADLs, transfers, mobility, fall precautions, environmental safety, emergency plans, pain management, infection control measures, skin
integrity, and energy-conserving measures. SN also educated the patient on signs and symptoms to report to the MD/SN, as well as signs and symptoms requiring an immediate ER visit or calling
911. SN followed COVID-19 infection precaution guidelines for healthcare professionals. Patient instructed on CDC-provided COVID-19 prevention measures. Patient denies symptoms of SOB,
cough, high fever, or sore throat at this visit or in the last 48 hours. Patient verbalized understanding of all teachings.

Patient Care Plan I Goals / Interventions:
Patient's vital signs will be within normal limits.
Check O2 saturation every visit for any shortness of breath on exertion, fatigue/weakness. Check VS every visit and report to PMD if patient vitals are not within the parameters.
Demonstrate pursed-lip breathing exercises, deep breathing exercises, and energy-conserving measures.
Review patient/caregiver on methods to conserve energy, frequent rest periods, sitting instead of standing to perform tasks, and performing slower, purposeful movements which will
limit fatigue. Instruct patient/caregiver on pursed lip breathing exercises, deep breathing exercises to alleviate dyspnea.
Patient will be free from signs/symptoms of cardiac and respiratory distress during the certification period. Verbalize understanding of symptoms of cardiac complications and when to call 911.
Reinforce patient/caregiver on sodium limitations, managing stress, avoiding smoking/alcohol intake, and compliance with prescribed medications to control blood pressure. Encourage
the patient/caregiver to provide a quiet environment, limiting stimuli to promote rest. Advise the patient to get up slowly to offset the feeling of dizziness. Instruct patient/caregiver to notify
SN/MD for signs/symptoms of cardiac and respiratory distress like chest pain, cyanosis, dependent edema, jugular vein distention, palpitations, wheezing, increased respiratory rate, anxiety,
headache, lightheadedness, and shortness of breath.
Patient will be free from cardiopulmonary distress during the care episode. Patient's pulse and blood pressure will be within established parameters.
Reinforce patient/caregiver to check and record blood pressure, including heart rate. Assess cardiopulmonary status every visit.
Problem: Bilateral primary osteoarthritis of knee
Reduce pain and improve mobility in the knees
Utilize heat and cold therapy to manage pain and swelling in the knees.
Regularly assess pain levels and functional mobility to adjust the care plan as needed.
Enhance activities of daily living (ADLs) and independence
Conduct home safety assessments to minimize fall risks and enhance mobility.
Schedule regular follow-ups to monitor progress and make necessary adjustments.
Educate patient and family about dietary changes to support joint health
Provide dietary counseling focused on anti-inflammatory foods and weight management.
Encourage hydration and its role in joint health and overall well-being.
Monitor the patient's weight and adjust the diet plan as necessary.
Educate the patient and family on the importance of elevation of the affected limb.
Utilize compression garments to manage swelling.
Monitor and assess limb measurements regularly to track progress.
Enhance patient and family understanding of lymphedema management.
Provide educational materials on lymphedema and self-care techniques.
Conduct family meetings to discuss care plans and expectations.
Facilitate support groups or counseling for emotional support.
Educate on proper skin care and hygiene in the affected area.
Coordinate care with healthcare providers for any comorbid conditions.
Develop a personalized care plan that includes emergency contacts and procedures.
Problem: Hypertensive chronic kidney disease with stages 1-4/unsp chronic kidney disease
Maintain blood pressure within normal ranges.
Monitor and record blood pressure daily.
Provide education on blood pressure management and medication adherence.
Develop a personalized medication schedule for antihypertensive drugs.
Prevent progression of chronic kidney disease.
Assess kidney function through regular lab testing.
Educate the patient and family about the importance of hydration and fluid management.
Encourage regular follow-up appointments with a nephrologist.
Enhance overall physical health and mobility.
Monitor and reassess physical capabilities regularly.
Maintain stable kidney function and prevent progression of the disease.
Monitor blood pressure and renal function through regular assessments.
Educate the patient and family about the importance of adhering to prescribed medications and diet.
Facilitate regular follow-up appointments with the nephrologist.
Enhance patient and family education regarding chronic kidney disease management.
Conduct educational sessions for the patient and family about CKD and treatment options.
Provide resources and materials on CKD management to the patient and family.
Assess the patient's understanding of their condition and address any misconceptions.
Manage co-morbid conditions effectively to reduce the burden on kidney function.
Coordinate care for diabetes or hypertension management if applicable.
Implement medication management strategies to avoid nephrotoxic drugs.
Schedule regular lab tests to monitor electrolyte levels and kidney function.
Problem: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, unspecified
Improve respiratory function and reduce symptoms
Conduct regular pulmonary function tests to monitor lung capacity and function.
Educate the patient and family on proper inhaler techniques and medication adherence.
Monitor environmental triggers that may exacerbate respiratory issues and provide avoidance strategies.
Enhance overall physical endurance and strength
Conduct regular mobility assessments to adjust exercises as needed.
Manage nutritional needs to support respiratory health
Assess nutritional intake and provide dietary recommendations to support lung health.
Monitor weight and nutritional status regularly to detect changes early.
Problem: Chronic respiratory failure with hypoxia.
Administer supplemental oxygen as prescribed to maintain SpO2 above 92%.
Monitor respiratory status and oxygen saturation levels regularly.
Educate the patient and family on proper use of oxygen equipment.
Enhance the patient's overall physical endurance.
Monitor and adjust the exercise program based on patient's tolerance.
Provide education to the family on recognizing signs of fatigue.
Manage and minimize exacerbations of respiratory failure.
Educate the patient and family on medication management and adherence.
Schedule regular follow-up visits to monitor the patient's condition.
Reduce the risk of falls in the home environment.
Conduct a home safety assessment to identify and mitigate fall hazards.
Educate the patient and family on fall prevention strategies.
Review and adjust medications that may contribute to fall risk.
Enhance the patient's awareness of their surroundings and mobility.
Provide education on recognizing and reporting symptoms of dizziness or instability.
Encourage regular eye exams to assess vision changes that may affect balance.
Monitor and document any fall incidents to adjust the care plan accordingly.
Improve the patient's strength and balance through targeted exercises.
Schedule regular follow-up visits to assess progress and modify the exercise plan.
Verbalize understanding of the importance of pressure-relieving and proper incontinence care measures.
Instruct caregiver to change patient's pads/underwear frequently or as needed when heavily soiled to prevent skin breakdown related to excessive moisture from urine/stool. Instruct
patient/caregiver to cleanse skin with mild soap and water then pat dry thoroughly after every incontinence. Instruct patient to wipe perineum from front to back to prevent infection. Instruct
patient/caregiver on pressure-relieving measures such as repositioning every 2 hours, float heels, pad bony prominences and methods to reduce friction and shear.
Instruct patient/caregiver to keep skin clean and dry to prevent skin breakdown. Instruct patient/caregiver to inspect skin daily and notify SN/MD for redness or open sores. Assess skin
for breakdown due to risk of pressure ulcer development.
Problem: Rehospitalization Risk and Medication Management
Patient will be free from side effects and adverse medication reactions during the entire episode. Patient/Caregiver will demonstrate medication safety and compliance. Patients will have no re-
Identify Medication Interactions and evaluate patient/family/caregiver(s) understanding of medication compliance.
